refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative worksite trials; if p95 ≤ CT_target, the FR is satisfied. If p95 > CT_target, investigate whether the failure is due to (a) inadequate DP (design cannot support the target) or (b) operational/environmental factors outside the DP scope.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to load material within the allocated load-phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to travel (loaded and unloaded) within the allocated travel-phase time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to dump material within the allocated dump-phase time budget.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independent sub-processes (load, travel, dump) with dedicated control logic and resource allocation per phase, enabling sequential execution with phase-specific optimization.

DP2: Implement a unified state machine that orchestrates all cycle phases through a single control entity, with phase transitions triggered by sensor feedback and time-based guards, ensuring deterministic sequencing.

DP3: Allocate cycle time budget across phases (load_time, travel_time, dump_time) with real-time monitoring and adaptive phase duration adjustment based on measured vs. target performance, maintaining p95 compliance through feedback control.

DP4: Design a resource-constrained scheduler that prioritizes cycle-critical operations (load, travel, dump) over secondary tasks, with preemption rules and priority queues to guarantee cycle time under site duty conditions.

DP5: Establish a cycle-time contract layer that decouples phase execution from underlying subsystem implementations, allowing each phase to be realized independently (mechanical, hydraulic, or software) while guaranteeing phase completion within allocated time windows.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3 + DP5 (Integrated): Allocate cycle time budget across phases with real-time monitoring and adaptive adjustment (DP3), realized through a cycle-time contract layer (DP5) that decouples phase execution from subsystem implementations. This approach enables: (1) explicit time budgeting per phase, (2) feedback-driven adaptation to site conditions, (3) independent subsystem realization, and (4) measurable p95 compliance through instrumentation.

potential_sources: DP3 and DP5 are complementary: DP5 provides the architectural boundary (contract), DP3 provides the control logic. Risk: if phase contracts are not clearly defined, adaptive adjustment (DP3) may violate subsystem assumptions.
Coupling risk between load and travel phases if load completion is not deterministic; mitigation: explicit handoff signal and timeout guard.
Coupling risk between travel and dump phases if travel distance/terrain varies; mitigation: adaptive travel time budget within allocated window.
Coupling risk with site duty cycle: if duty cycle assumptions change, phase budgets must be re-validated; mitigation: parameterized time budgets and re-baselining procedure.
coupling_check.notes: DP3+DP5 avoids tight coupling by introducing explicit phase contracts and time budgets. Each phase can be optimized independently within its time window. Feedback control (DP3) operates at the cycle orchestration level, not within phase implementations, preserving subsystem autonomy. Coupling is managed through well-defined handoff signals and timeout guards.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Phase completion times are measurable and repeatable under site conditions. RISK: Terrain variability, load mass variation, or hydraulic temperature drift may cause phase duration variance. MITIGATION: Instrumentation and statistical analysis (p95 baseline); adaptive time budgets.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle-time contract can be enforced across heterogeneous subsystems (mechanical, hydraulic, software). RISK: Subsystem response times may not be deterministic (e.g., hydraulic lag, mechanical friction). MITIGATION: Characterize subsystem response curves; include safety margins in phase budgets.

ASSUMPTION: Real-time monitoring and feedback control can adjust phase durations without violating safety constraints. RISK: Aggressive time reduction may compromise load stability or operator safety. MITIGATION: Define safety-critical phase boundaries; enforce minimum phase durations.

ASSUMPTION: Site duty cycle and operating conditions are stable and known at design time. RISK: Unexpected site conditions (extreme temperature, high altitude, soft terrain) may invalidate phase budgets. MITIGATION: Parameterized budgets; on-site re-baselining procedure.

ASSUMPTION: p95 metric is achievable with current subsystem capabilities. RISK: If subsystem latencies are inherently high, p95 target may be unattainable. MITIGATION: Early subsystem performance validation; trade-off analysis (cycle time vs. subsystem cost/complexity).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Phase-level acceptance criteria (e.g., load_time_p95, travel_time_p95, dump_time_p95) to enable independent subsystem design and validation. (2) Duty cycle profile (load mass range, travel distance range, terrain type, ambient conditions) to ground phase budget allocation. (3) Safety constraints that bound phase duration reduction (e.g., minimum load stability margin, operator reaction time). (4) Instrumentation and feedback control scope (which phases are monitored? which are adaptive?). Revised FR: 'The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle with p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target under the specified duty cycle (load mass 5–25 t, travel distance 50–500 m, terrain type X), with phase-level budgets [load: t_L, travel: t_T, dump: t_D] and safety margins [stability: ±5%, operator reaction: ±2 s]. Real-time monitoring shall enable adaptive phase duration adjustment within safety bounds to maintain p95 compliance across site condition variance.'

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Cycle-time contract layer and adaptive budget allocation engine that orchestrates phase execution timing, monitors real-time cycle progress, and adjusts phase allocations based on site telemetry feedback. Owns the cycle-time state machine, budget ledger, and adaptation logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound phase progress, subsystem readiness, and site condition signals required for real-time monitoring and adaptive adjustment triggers.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound phase execution commands and time-window constraints to Vehicle and Teleoperation, enforcing the allocated cycle-time budget per phase.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety constraint signals: inbound alerts that may trigger budget reallocation; outbound cycle-time compliance status for safety validation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the orchestration layer naturally responsible for cross-phase timing coordination, budget allocation logic, and adaptive control. The cycle-time contract and monitoring instrumentation are core to solution-level orchestration. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry inbound and Tasking outbound cross the boundary; SafetyAlert integration is localized.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a subsystem executor, not an orchestrator. Placing cycle-time budgeting and cross-phase adaptation logic here would scatter orchestration concerns across subsystems. High diffusion: Teleoperation, Safety Services, and other subsystems would need to query or subscribe to Vehicle's budget state, violating separation of concerns.

Poor containment. Teleoperation is a control interface, not a timing orchestrator. Embedding cycle-time contracts and adaptive budgeting here conflates user control with system-level timing governance. High diffusion: Vehicle and Safety Services would depend on Teleoperation for timing decisions, creating inappropriate coupling.

Weak containment. Safety Services owns safety constraints and validation, not cycle-time orchestration. While SafetyAlert integration is natural, the core DP (budget allocation, phase sequencing, adaptive adjustment) belongs at solution level. Placing it here would entangle safety logic with timing governance, reducing clarity and testability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of the integrated DP3+DP5 because it is the system-level orchestrator responsible for coordinating phase execution across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). The cycle-time contract layer is a solution-level abstraction that decouples phase timing from subsystem implementations—a responsibility that belongs at the top level, not within any single subsystem. Real-time monitoring and adaptive adjustment are orchestration concerns that require visibility across all phases and subsystems, which only the solution layer possesses.
containment_risks: Risk: If Telemetry signals are delayed or incomplete, the NGHE Solution's adaptive adjustment logic may make suboptimal budget reallocations. Mitigation: Define Telemetry SLA and implement fallback heuristics.
Risk: Tasking interface may become a bottleneck if phase commands are issued synchronously. Mitigation: Use asynchronous event-driven tasking with acknowledgment callbacks.

Risk: SafetyAlert bidirectional coupling could create circular dependencies if Safety Services queries NGHE Solution's budget state while NGHE Solution waits for SafetyAlert responses. Mitigation: Establish clear event ordering: SafetyAlert inbound → budget reallocation → Tasking outbound.
Risk: Instrumentation for p95 compliance monitoring may introduce overhead if not carefully designed. Mitigation: Use sampling or aggregation to minimize telemetry volume.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 + DP5 (Integrated) directly addresses the FR requirement to enable load→travel→dump cycle execution within customer-defined cycle time target. The integrated approach of allocating cycle time budget across phases (DP3) with real-time monitoring and adaptive adjustment via a cycle-time contract layer (DP5) is logically sound and provides explicit mechanisms for: (1) phase-level time budgeting, (2) feedback-driven adaptation to site variability, and (3) measurable p95 compliance. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide design and is traceable to the acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target).

diffusion_risks: Telemetry inbound interface: If phase progress signals are delayed, incomplete, or inconsistent across subsystems, the NGHE Solution's adaptive adjustment logic may operate on stale state. Mitigation required: Define Telemetry SLA (latency, completeness, consistency guarantees) and implement fallback heuristics for missing signals.

Tasking outbound interface: Synchronous phase command issuance could create bottlenecks or deadlocks if Vehicle or Teleoperation do not acknowledge within expected time windows. Mitigation required: Implement asynchronous event-driven tasking with explicit acknowledgment callbacks and timeout handling.

SafetyAlert bidirectional coupling: Circular dependency risk if Safety Services queries NGHE Solution's budget state while NGHE Solution awaits SafetyAlert responses before reallocating budget. Mitigation required: Establish strict event ordering protocol (SafetyAlert inbound → budget reallocation decision → Tasking outbound) and avoid synchronous cross-queries.

Instrumentation overhead: Real-time monitoring and p95 compliance tracking may introduce telemetry volume that degrades system responsiveness if not carefully designed. Mitigation required: Use sampling, aggregation, or asynchronous logging to minimize impact on cycle-time execution.

Minimal but present diffusion: While NGHE Solution containment score is 0.92 (excellent), the 0.08 diffusion score indicates that Telemetry inbound and Tasking outbound interfaces are necessary boundary crossings. Ensure these interfaces are well-specified with clear contracts (message schemas, timing guarantees, error handling) to prevent implicit dependencies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for the integrated DP3+DP5. It is the system-level orchestrator naturally responsible for cross-phase timing coordination, budget allocation logic, and adaptive control. The cycle-time contract layer is a solution-level abstraction that decouples phase timing from subsystem implementations—a responsibility that cannot be delegated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services without violating separation of concerns and creating high diffusion. The module containment analysis confirms this: Vehicle (0.35), Teleoperation (0.3), and Safety Services (0.4) all exhibit poor containment and high diffusion (0.65, 0.7, 0.6 respectively) if assigned this DP. NGHE Solution's 0.92 containment score reflects its natural role as the orchestration layer with visibility across all phases and subsystems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The IM boundary (NGHE Solution) owns the cycle-time state machine, budget ledger, and adaptation logic—all directly observable and measurable. Verification of the FR (cycle time p95 ≤ CT_target) is feasible through: (1) instrumentation of phase execution timestamps within NGHE Solution, (2) collection of Telemetry signals to validate real-time monitoring, (3) analysis of budget allocation decisions and adaptive adjustments, and (4) statistical analysis of measured cycle times across the agreed duty cycle and site conditions. The acceptance criterion is directly traceable to IM-level observables.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to a single, well-defined IM (NGHE Solution) with explicit ownership of cycle-time orchestration. The external interfaces (Telemetry inbound, Tasking outbound, SafetyAlert bidirectional) are clearly specified, enabling unambiguous allocation of responsibilities to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services as subsystem executors. The cycle-time contract layer provides a clear abstraction boundary between orchestration (NGHE Solution) and execution (subsystems), reducing ambiguity about who is responsible for what aspect of cycle-time management.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry SLA: Define latency bounds (e.g., max 50 ms), completeness guarantees (e.g., all phase progress signals must be present), and consistency rules (e.g., monotonic timestamp ordering). Document fallback heuristics for missing or delayed signals.

Specify Tasking interface protocol: Define message schema, asynchronous acknowledgment mechanism, timeout handling, and retry logic. Ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation can reject or negotiate time-window constraints with explicit feedback to NGHE Solution.

Establish SafetyAlert event ordering: Document the strict sequence: (1) SafetyAlert inbound received, (2) NGHE Solution evaluates impact on budget, (3) budget reallocation decision made, (4) Tasking outbound issued with updated constraints, (5) SafetyAlert outbound status sent. Prohibit synchronous cross-queries between NGHE Solution and Safety Services.

Design instrumentation for minimal overhead: Specify sampling rate, aggregation strategy, and asynchronous logging to ensure telemetry collection does not degrade cycle-time execution. Define p95 compliance monitoring as a post-execution analysis, not a real-time blocking operation.

Add interface contract specifications: Create detailed interface control documents (ICDs) for Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert that include message formats, timing guarantees, error codes, and expected behavior under nominal and off-nominal conditions.

Define adaptive adjustment triggers and bounds: Specify the conditions under which NGHE Solution reallocates budget (e.g., phase exceeds 80% of allocated time), the maximum reallocation per cycle, and the constraints that must be respected (e.g., minimum time per phase for safety). This prevents unbounded or oscillatory adjustments.

Validate cycle-time contract feasibility: Conduct a design review to confirm that the allocated phase time budgets are achievable given subsystem capabilities and site conditions. Use historical data or simulation to validate p95 compliance under expected variability.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The integrated DP3+DP5 is logically sound, the NGHE Solution IM boundary is correct, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results identify six critical interface and design risks that require explicit decomposition into next-level sub-FRs to ensure safe, robust realization. These risks are not showstoppers but demand architectural rigor: (1) Telemetry SLA and fallback heuristics, (2) Tasking protocol with asynchronous acknowledgment, (3) SafetyAlert event ordering and circular dependency prevention, (4) Instrumentation overhead minimization, (5) Adaptive adjustment bounds and triggers, and (6) Cycle-time contract feasibility validation. Decomposing these into sub-FRs ensures each risk is addressed with explicit DPs and IMs, preventing diffusion and implicit dependencies. Given high safety criticality, this rigor is mandatory.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-1.1: Ensure Telemetry inbound signals (phase progress, subsystem state) are received with bounded latency (≤50 ms), completeness (all required signals present), and consistency (monotonic ordering) to enable real-time budget adaptation without stale-state errors.

FR-NGHE-1.2: Implement asynchronous Tasking outbound protocol with explicit acknowledgment callbacks, timeout handling, and rejection/negotiation feedback from Vehicle and Teleoperation to prevent synchronous bottlenecks and deadlocks.

FR-NGHE-1.3: Establish strict SafetyAlert event ordering (inbound → budget evaluation → reallocation decision → Tasking outbound → status response) and prohibit synchronous cross-queries between NGHE Solution and Safety Services to eliminate circular dependency risk.

FR-NGHE-1.4: Design real-time monitoring and p95 compliance instrumentation with minimal overhead (sampling, aggregation, asynchronous logging) such that telemetry collection does not degrade cycle-time execution latency.

FR-NGHE-1.5: Define adaptive cycle-time budget reallocation logic with explicit triggers (e.g., phase exceeds 80% of allocated time), maximum reallocation bounds per cycle, and safety-critical constraints (e.g., minimum time per phase) to prevent unbounded or oscillatory adjustments.

FR-NGHE-1.6: Validate that allocated phase time budgets are achievable given subsystem capabilities, site variability, and duty cycle conditions, with statistical evidence (historical data or simulation) that p95 cycle-time compliance is attainable.

revise.why: No revisions to current-level FR, DP, or IM are required. The gates confirm logical soundness and correct ownership. Decomposition into sub-FRs is the appropriate next step to address interface and design risks.

assumptions: Telemetry signals from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems are available and can be instrumented with bounded latency.
Tasking commands can be issued asynchronously with acknowledgment callbacks; synchronous blocking is not required for safety.
SafetyAlert events are infrequent enough that strict event ordering does not create unacceptable latency in budget reallocation.
Instrumentation overhead can be minimized to <5% of cycle-time budget through sampling and asynchronous logging.
Adaptive reallocation logic can be designed with clear bounds and triggers that prevent oscillation and maintain safety margins.
Historical data or simulation tools are available to validate cycle-time contract feasibility before deployment.
open_risks: Telemetry SLA not yet formalized: If latency, completeness, or consistency guarantees are not explicitly defined and enforced, NGHE Solution's adaptive logic may operate on stale or incomplete state, leading to missed cycle-time targets or safety violations.

Tasking protocol not yet specified: Lack of clear asynchronous acknowledgment mechanism and timeout handling could result in deadlocks or undetected command failures, causing phase execution to stall.

SafetyAlert circular dependency: If synchronous cross-queries between NGHE Solution and Safety Services are not prohibited, circular wait conditions could occur, blocking budget reallocation and delaying phase execution.
Instrumentation overhead unquantified: Real-time monitoring may consume significant CPU or network bandwidth, degrading cycle-time execution if not carefully designed.

Adaptive adjustment bounds not defined: Without explicit triggers and maximum reallocation limits, the system could oscillate or violate safety-critical time constraints (e.g., minimum dwell time for load stability).
Cycle-time contract feasibility not validated: Allocated phase budgets may be unachievable given subsystem capabilities or site variability, leading to systematic p95 compliance failures.
evidence_needed: Telemetry SLA specification document with latency bounds, completeness guarantees, consistency rules, and fallback heuristics for missing/delayed signals.
Tasking interface control document (ICD) with message schema, asynchronous acknowledgment protocol, timeout values, retry logic, and rejection/negotiation feedback mechanism.
SafetyAlert event ordering protocol document with strict sequence diagram, prohibition on synchronous cross-queries, and timeout handling for each event transition.
Instrumentation design specification with sampling rate, aggregation strategy, asynchronous logging mechanism, and measured overhead impact (<5% of cycle-time budget).
Adaptive reallocation logic specification with explicit triggers (e.g., phase time thresholds), maximum reallocation bounds per cycle, and safety-critical constraints (e.g., minimum phase durations).
Cycle-time contract feasibility analysis report with historical data or simulation results demonstrating p95 compliance under expected duty cycle and site variability conditions.
